title: Plasma Glucose -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings
content: The diagnosis of DM has serious consequences. DM confers a risk of long-term diabetic complications, including blindness, renal failure, and amputations, as well as an increased risk of cardiovascular disease. The condition also entails a lifetime of dietary restrictions and medications and can seriously curtail lifestyle and employment prospects. [37] The diagnosis may be suggested by the patient’s history or by the results of dipstick tests for glucose on urine specimens. However, urine glucose measurements are inadequate for diagnosing DM. [63] They potentially yield false-positive results in subjects with a low renal threshold for glucose. A patient with DM may deliver false-negative results if the patient is fasting. [64]

title: Blood Glucose Monitoring -- Clinical Significance -- Hyperglycemia
content: Symptoms of hyperglycemia include polyuria (increased and frequent urination), polydipsia (increased thirst), blurred vision, headache, fatigue, and glucosuria. Acute symptoms of hyperglycemia are not usually seen at levels below 14 mmol/L or 250 mg/dL.

title: Physiology, Glycosuria -- Issues of Concern
content: One limitation of urine glucose testing is the ability of most commercial semiquantitative urine tests to detect glucose in the urine only until it reaches a level of 50 to 250 mg/dl. Also, errors can stem from an altered renal threshold. There are known variations in the renal threshold among individuals that can lead to significantly misleading data.

title: Blood Glucose Monitoring -- Clinical Significance -- Hyperglycemia
content: Untreated hyperglycemia may also result in a hyperglycemic hyperosmolar state. This is a rare condition seen most commonly in clients with type 2 diabetes mellitus. Profound hyperglycemia can result in significant glucosuria; because glucose is hydrophilic, the kidney produces exceedingly large volumes of urine, resulting in life-threatening dehydration and potential coma. Both diabetic ketoacidosis and hyperosmolar state require emergent management to reduce elevated blood glucose levels with insulin therapy.

content: Clients across the lifespan with diabetes mellitus have varying clinical presentations and underlying clinical pathologies. Clients with renal insufficiency are at risk of hypoglycemia as the kidneys are primarily responsible for metabolizing exogenous insulin.

Hence sole monitoring of the interstitial fluid may not always be a reliable indicator of rapidly changing blood glucose levels. title: Nocturia -- Evaluation -- Additional Diagnostic Studies
content: Nocturia is diagnosed based on clinical history; however, additional diagnostic studies are performed to assess for the underlying etiology and comorbid conditions. Serum glucose hemoglobin A1C, renal function, urinary osmolality, and serum electrolytes should be evaluated. A urinalysis should also be done. Patients with poorly controlled diabetes, renal disease, or nephrogenic diabetes insipidus often report nocturia. A formal postvoid residual volume with a straight catheterization or bladder scan should be performed. A postvoid residual of more than 200 mL is considered pathological and may require further evaluation. Patients with urinary retention and overflow can present with frequency and nocturia. [4] [20] [21] [22] [23] Cystoscopy and urodynamics are not generally necessary or recommended for most patients with nocturia. [4]

title: Physiology, Glycosuria -- Introduction
content: Small amounts of glucose in the urine are considered normal, but glucosuria usually refers to pathologic conditions where the amounts of urine glucose are more than 25 mg/dl in random fresh urine. The renal tubule normally reabsorbs almost all (leaving less than 25 mg/dl urine glucose) in the normal glomerular filtrate. When the glucose filtrated by the glomerular exceeds the capacity of the renal tubule to absorb it, the loss of balance occurs. It can happen due to elevated plasma glucose, as in diabetes mellitus, or when the ability of the tubule to absorb glucose is impaired, eg, Fanconi syndrome with impairment in the absorption of phosphate, amino acids, or isolated glucosuria as an inherited disorder termed Familial Renal glucosuria.

content: Fluoride ions in high concentration inhibit the activity of urease and certain other enzymes; consequently, the specimens are unsuitable for determining urea in procedures requiring urease and for the direct assay of some serum enzymes. [46] Potassium oxalate (K 2 C 2 O 4 ) causes cell water loss, diluting the plasma. Therefore, samples collected in these tubes should not be used to measure analytes other than glucose. [47] Although fluoride maintains long-term blood glucose stability, the rate of decline in the first hour after sample collection is not altered, and glycolysis may continue for up to 4 hours. [48]

title: Physiology, Glycosuria -- Introduction
content: Glycosuria is a term that defines the presence of reducing sugars in the urine, such as glucose, galactose, lactose, fructose, etc. Glucosuria connotes the presence of glucose in the urine and is the most frequent type of glycosuria, which is the focus of this review. It happens when the glomerulus filters more glucose than the proximal tubule can reabsorb. In normal individuals, glucosuria can be up to 0.25 mg/ml. More than 0.25 mg/ml in random fresh urine is considered increased glucosuria and can be due to elevated plasma glucose, renal glucose absorption impairment, or both. [1] [2] Physiologic glucosuria is a condition where individuals consume an excessive amount of carbohydrates.

title: Blood Glucose Monitoring -- Pathophysiology
content: In conditions like diabetes mellitus, there is either a lack of insulin or the body does not appropriately respond to the actions of insulin; the latter is termed insulin resistance, wherein cellular uptake of glucose or storage of excess glucose is impaired. Dysfunction in the production or uptake of insulin can potentiate impaired blood glucose levels. Patients with impaired blood glucose homeostasis and elevated fasting blood glucose are at high risk for developing diabetes mellitus. Patients may be diagnosed with diabetes mellitus if their blood glucose levels are high. [5] Some organs, such as the brain, kidneys, liver, and erythrocytes, do not have insulin receptors and do not require insulin for glucose uptake. These organs, especially the brain, are significantly affected by acute, chronic, or recurrent drops in blood glucose levels; morbidity in such situations may be significant. [6] [7]

title: Physiology, Glycosuria -- Pathophysiology
content: Glucosuria can also occur in increased glucose concentration in the circulating blood. This phenomenon can also occur in normal individuals who consume excess carbohydrates, known as ‘alimentary glycosuria.’ It also presents in diabetic patients. [8] In diabetes mellitus, glomeruli can be damaged with increasing duration, resulting in albuminuria and a decrease in the glomerular filtration rate. In diabetic patients, the kidneys are more susceptible to the effects of hyperglycemia; many of the kidney cells cannot decrease glucose transport rates and prevent intercellular hyperglycemia in an increased glucose concentration state. [7]

title: Diabetes Mellitus Screening -- Definition/Introduction -- Type 2 Diabetes
content: Type 2 diabetes may initially have few symptoms; however, long-term effects lead to a myriad of different problems late in the course of the disease, leading to debilitating sequelae. More than 30 million people in the US have diabetes. [3] Due to an initial silent course, it often remains undetected for quite a long time before the appearance of symptoms. The screening for the disease aims at prevention and early treatment with the assumption that there is a long-term benefit to its identification early in the disease process. The long-term benefit of screening vs. clinically detected diabetes is being explored. [4] Several studies with weak evidence suggest the merits of screening tests. [5] Screening for type 2 diabetes can be done by fasting blood sugar, hemoglobin (A1C), glucose tolerance testing, and random plasma sugar. Urine glucose may be useful, but it is an inferior test to screen for diabetes, although research is reconsidering this claim. [6]

title: Hyperglycemia -- Evaluation
content: When evaluating a patient for hyperglycemia, the focus should be on the patient's cardiorespiratory status, mental status, and volume status. Bedside serum glucose can be obtained quickly. Testing includes serum electrolytes with the calculation of the anion gap, blood urea nitrogen and creatinine, and complete blood count. Urinalysis by dipstick assesses glucose and ketones in the urine. Arterial blood gas or venous blood gas may be necessary if serum bicarbonate is substantially reduced. [9]

title: Glucose Intolerance -- Evaluation -- Fasting plasma glucose (FPG)
content: This test gives an average of blood glucose over last two to three months. Patients with A1C between 5.7% to 6.4% (39 to 47 mmol/mol) are diagnosed as having increased risk of diabetes or prediabetes, the latter has stigmatization and should be avoided where possible. For a diagnosis of diabetes, the A1C levels should be greater than or equal to 6.5% (48 mmol/mol). A1C is a convenient, standardized test but is costly and has many issues as discussed below including lower sensitivity. For all of the above tests, if the person is asymptomatic testing should be repeated on another occasion to make a diagnosis of diabetes.

title: Plasma Glucose -- Clinical Significance
content: Measurements of plasma glucose levels are essential for screening metabolic dysregulation, pre-diabetes, and diabetes. Evidence finds that the onset of type 2 diabetes can occur as early as 4 to 7 years before clinical diagnosis. Additionally, plasma glucose levels can monitor diabetes, screen for hypoglycemic episodes, guide treatment or lifestyle interventions, and predict risk for comorbidities like cardiovascular or eye and kidney disease. [78] In particular, evidence reports that plasma glucose level monitoring in patients with type 2 diabetes can predict complications and mortality. Higher glucose correlates with more significant comorbidities and risk for mortality. [79]

title: Glucose Intolerance -- Evaluation -- Fasting plasma glucose (FPG)
content: In this test, plasma glucose level is measured before and 2 hours after ingestion of 75 gm of glucose. IGT is diagnosed if plasma glucose (PG) level in the 2-hour sample is between 140 to 199 mg/dL (7.8 to 11.0 mmol/L). DM is diagnosed if the PG is greater than or equal to 200 mg/dl. It is also a standard test but is inconvenient and more costly than FPG and has major variability issues. Patients need to consume a diet with at least 150 g per day of carbohydrates for 3 to 5 days and not take any medications that can impact glucose tolerance such as steroids and thiazide diuretics. title: Physiology, Glycosuria -- Mechanism
content: The renal system filters approximately 180 g of glucose daily in healthy individuals. The glucose entering the tubular system is reabsorbed along with the PCT segments. In diabetic patients, as a result of the increase in plasma glucose, the filtered glucose exceeds the capacity of the tubular system and results in glucosuria. Most glucose uptake of over 90% occurs in the proximal tubule, mediated by SGLT2, a low-affinity/high-capacity transporter. The proximal tubule's distal parts reabsorb the remaining glucose via the high-affinity/low-capacity SGLT1. [6] Kidneys play a significant role in maintaining glucose homeostasis and preventing an individual from developing hypoglycemia. Maintaining glucose homeostasis by the kidney includes glucose reabsorption in the PCT,  gluconeogenesis, and the clearance of important hormones such as insulin. [7]

title: Diabetes -- Evaluation
content: Several lab tests are useful in the management of chronic DM. Home glucose testing can show trends of hyper- and hypoglycemia. The HbA1c test indicates the extent of glycation due to hyperglycemia over three months (the life of the red blood cell). Urine albumin testing can identify the early stages of diabetic nephropathy. Since patients with diabetes are also prone to cardiovascular disease, serum lipid monitoring is advisable at the time of diagnosis. Similarly, some recommend monitoring thyroid status by obtaining a blood level of thyroid-stimulating hormone annually due to a higher incidence of hypothyroidism. [24] [25]

title: Blood Glucose Monitoring -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings -- Diabetes
content: Further testing may involve an oral glucose tolerance test to confirm the diagnosis. Advise the client to eat and drink over 150 grams per day of carbohydrate foods for the three days before testing. In addition, the client must fast overnight for at least 8 to 16 hours before this test. A fasting blood sample is collected, and a sweet drink containing 75 grams of glucose is given to the client after collecting the fasting blood sample. A second blood sample is collected two hours after consuming the glucose drink. Oral glucose tolerance test: Glucose tolerance range at two hours post 75-gram oral glucose ingestion: ≥11.1 mmol/L, or ≥200 mg/dL.

title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2157
content: Increased plasma osmolality is clinically associated with elevated blood glucose levels (hyperglycemia). This rise in plasma osmolality often originates from dehydration, as fluid loss concentrates solutes in the bloodstream. Additionally, increased plasma osmolality commonly co‑occurs with low blood volume (hypovolemia). Recognizing that dehydration can lead to higher plasma osmolality, which in turn may signal hyperglycemia and accompany hypovolemia, is essential for accurate diagnosis and appropriate fluid and glucose management in patient care.

This condition is also linked to urinary tract disorders (اضطراب السبيل البولي), suggesting that disturbances of the urinary system can be an underlying or related factor in the development of high plasma osmolality. Additionally, increased plasma osmolality frequently co‑occurs with low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم), reflecting the close interplay between osmotic balance, fluid volume status, and glucose regulation. Recognizing that hyperglycemia, urinary tract pathology, and hypovolemia commonly intersect with elevated plasma osmolality helps clinicians assess underlying causes and guide comprehensive patient management.

Diabetes is clinically associated with retinal disorder (اضطراب في الشبكية) and more specifically with diabetic retinopathy (اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ). Both diabetes and diabetic retinopathy are also clinically associated with end‑stage renal disease (مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية). These connections illustrate how diabetes can lead to multiple organ complications, linking retinal pathology and kidney failure, and emphasize the need for integrated monitoring and management of patients with diabetes.

Both conditions represent severe microvascular complications of long‑standing diabetes mellitus, and the presence of diabetic retinopathy often signals an increased risk of progression to advanced kidney failure. Recognizing this association is essential for clinicians, as early detection and management of retinal changes can prompt more aggressive monitoring of renal function, and coordinated care can help mitigate the morbidity associated with simultaneous vision loss and the need for renal replacement therapy.

title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1687
content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري من النوع الثاني (2) المتطلب للأنسولين), indicating that patients with this form of diabetes are at heightened risk for sudden deterioration of renal function. In the context of acute kidney failure, abnormalities in extrarenal nitrogenous blood (نيتروجينية الدم خارج الكلوية) may develop, reflecting the accumulation of nitrogenous waste products when the kidneys are unable to adequately filter the blood. Moreover, acute kidney failure serves as a parent condition for urinary tract disorders (اضطراب السبيل البولي), suggesting that severe renal impairment can predispose patients to complications of the urinary system. Together, these relationships underscore the importance of vigilant monitoring of renal health in insulin‑dependent type 2 diabetic patients, early detection of nitrogenous waste disturbances, and proactive management of potential urinary tract complications to improve overall patient outcomes.

title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 5 -- Community 2962
content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with type 2 diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري نوع 2) and with reduced blood volume (قلة حجم الدم). Type 2 diabetes can predispose patients to acute kidney injury through chronic hyperglycemia‑induced vascular and tubular damage, making the kidneys more vulnerable to hemodynamic stress. Conversely, hypovolemia decreases renal perfusion and can directly precipitate acute kidney failure. Recognizing that both type 2 diabetes and low intravascular volume are important clinical contributors to acute kidney failure guides clinicians to monitor diabetic patients closely, ensure adequate hydration, and intervene promptly to preserve renal function.

title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1875
content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that includes several related disorders: ارتفاع السكر في الدم (elevated blood glucose), مرض السكري دون مضاعفات (diabetes without complications), اعتلال عصبي سكري (diabetic neuropathy), فرط أنسولين الدم (hyperinsulinemia), and ورم غلوكاغوني (glucagonoma). These entities represent a spectrum of pancreatic endocrine dysfunction, ranging from simple hyperglycemia to specific complications such as neuropathy, as well as hormonal excess states like hyperinsulinemia and glucagon‑secreting tumors. Understanding that all of these conditions are subtypes of diseases of the endocrine pancreas, including diabetes, helps clinicians recognize the varied presentations, anticipate potential complications, and tailor comprehensive patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1624
content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with type 1 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 1) and with type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 2), including the insulin‑requiring form of type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري من النوع الثاني (2) المتطلب للأنسولين). These associations reflect the heightened risk of sudden renal impairment in patients who have chronic hyperglycemia and diabetic nephropathy. In addition, bladder cancer (سرطان المثانة) is clinically associated with acute kidney failure, indicating that malignancy of the urinary bladder may coexist with or contribute to rapid loss of kidney function. Acute kidney failure also has a specific subcategory described as extracorporeal blood nitrogen (نيتروجينية الدم خارج الكلوية), suggesting a particular physiological or therapeutic aspect—such as nitrogen handling during dialysis—that falls under the broader condition of acute kidney failure. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians monitor diabetic patients for rapid renal deterioration, evaluate bladder‑cancer patients for possible kidney complications, and consider specialized management strategies for the extracorporeal nitrogen component of acute kidney failure.

title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2745
content: High plasma osmolality, high blood glucose, acute kidney failure, type 2 diabetes, an abnormal psychological state, and low blood volume are closely interrelated clinical entities. High plasma osmolality is clinically associated with elevated blood glucose levels, reflecting the hyperosmolar consequences of uncontrolled diabetes, and it is also linked to type 2 diabetes itself, indicating that patients with this form of diabetes frequently develop increased plasma osmolality. In addition, high plasma osmolality is clinically associated with an abnormal psychological state, consistent with the neurocognitive disturbances that can arise from severe hyperosmolar conditions, and it commonly co‑occurs with low blood volume, suggesting that dehydration or hypovolemia often accompanies the hyperosmolar state. Acute kidney failure is clinically associated with type 2 diabetes, underscoring diabetes as a major risk factor for rapid loss of renal function, and it is also linked to an abnormal psychological state, reflecting the mental status changes that can result from uremic toxins. Moreover, acute kidney failure is clinically associated with low blood volume, indicating that hypovolemia can precipitate or worsen renal injury. Together, these relationships highlight a network in which type 2 diabetes can lead to high plasma osmolality and acute kidney failure, both of which are connected to low blood volume and neuropsychiatric disturbances, emphasizing the importance of monitoring osmolar status, volume balance, renal function, and mental health in patients with diabetes.
